description: This figure presents the evolution of retained austenite (RA) during thermomechanical processing of Fe-0.25C-3Mn-2Al-0.4Si low-density steel. Panels (a)–(d) are EBSD phase maps showing the spatial distribution of BCC (red) and FCC (green) phases, alongside tabulated phase fractions and RA particle-size histograms: the hot-rolled HR sample is fully BCC (FCC fraction 0); AN750 (intercritically annealed at 750°C) contains an FCC fraction of 0.030 with an average RA diameter of 0.59 μm; AN800 (800°C) shows an FCC fraction of 0.011 and an average diameter of 0.72 μm; AN850 (850°C) retains only an FCC fraction of 0.002 with an average diameter of 0.83 μm. Panel (e) quantifies RA content in volume percent, peaking at 3.0 vol% for AN750 and decreasing to 1.1 vol% for AN800 and 0.2 vol% for AN850. Panel (f) provides XRD patterns with indexed α (BCC) and γ (FCC) reflections, where γ peak intensities progressively diminish from AN750 to AN850. Collectively, the data demonstrate that increasing intercritical annealing temperature markedly reduces RA volume fraction while marginally increasing its average particle size, with the 750°C treatment yielding the highest RA content and stability.
